vitals.distance: Cu Chulainn 2,500 km; Sedanta 1,700 km; Cean Beag O Dheas 1,000 km; Cean Beag O Thuaidh 1,000 km
vitals.terrain: Mixed road terrain along Ireland's west coast

course_description.overview: A one-stage, self-supported ultra-endurance road cycling event traversing Ireland's Wild Atlantic Way from Cork to Derry. The 2026 edition (11th) features four route options ranging from 1,000 km to 2,500 km, navigating seven of Ireland's westernmost counties via iconic blue zig-zag signposts. Riders experience rugged Atlantic coastline, heritage landmarks, and welcoming villages throughout the journey.

key_segments.name: Cu Chulainn
key_segments.description: The longest and most demanding route spanning from Kinsale to Derry, representing the full Wild Atlantic Way experience

key_segments.name: Sedanta
key_segments.description: A slightly shorter yet equally challenging option from Kinsale to Derry

key_segments.name: Cean Beag O Dheas
key_segments.description: Southern half route covering the Wild Atlantic Way from Kinsale to Galway

key_segments.name: Cean Beag O Thuaidh
key_segments.description: Northern half route from Galway to Derry

course_description.difficulty_narrative: An extreme ultra-endurance challenge demanding total self-sufficiency and exceptional physical and mental resilience. The event combines significant distance and elevation gain with self-supported logistics, requiring riders to manage nutrition, navigation, and equipment independently. Previous editions saw top performers completing the Cu Chulainn route in six days and Sedanta in five days, indicating the intense pace required for competitive finishes.

logistics.getting_there: Event starts in Cork and finishes in Derry, Ireland. Participants must arrange their own transportation to the start location.
logistics.accommodation: Not included in entry fee. Optional group accommodations available at start and finish locations (details to be confirmed). Riders are responsible for securing lodging along the route.
logistics.registration_details: Entry fees: £480 for full routes (Cu Chulainn and Sedanta); £380 for half routes (Cean Beag O Dheas and Cean Beag O Thuaidh). Riders can enter as solo competitors or in pairs. Flexible route selection allows participants to mix and match routes as the ride progresses. Registration opened November 1, 2025.
logistics.bike_logistics: Bag and bike box drop-off service available from Cork start to Derry finish. All riders equipped with mandatory GPS tracker for real-time progress monitoring, route validation, and safety tracking. Tracker data monitored by race organizers and accessible to friends and family.
logistics.local_considerations: The event supports Foyle Search and Rescue, a charity operating along the River Foyle in Derry City. The route follows the Wild Atlantic Way through seven westernmost Irish counties, passing through heritage sites, villages, and coastal areas. Self-supported format requires riders to source food, water, and services from local communities along the route.

final_verdict.one_liner: An epic self-supported road cycling odyssey along Ireland's most dramatic coastline, combining extreme distance and elevation with notable scenery and personal challenge.
final_verdict.who_is_it_for: Experienced ultra-endurance cyclists seeking a transformative multi-day adventure. Solo riders or pairs comfortable with total self-sufficiency, navigation responsibility, and extended periods of independent cycling. Participants must possess strong mental resilience, route-finding capability, and self-management skills. Best suited for cyclists with previous long-distance touring or ultra-racing experience.
final_verdict.is_it_worth_it: Yes, for the right athlete. The TransAtlanticWay delivers an authentic, community-connected ultra-endurance experience through some of Europe's most stunning coastal terrain. The self-supported format ensures a uniquely personal journey. However, the high physical demands, logistical complexity, and moderate entry costs (£380-480) require serious commitment. The lack of accommodation inclusion and limited aid stations demand experienced self-sufficiency. Ideal for cyclists seeking prestige, adventure, and personal transformation rather than competitive racing.
pro_tips: Begin training 4-6 months in advance with long-distance road cycling and elevation-specific workouts; previous ultra-distance experience strongly recommended
Scout accommodation options early along the route; book lodging in advance for peak June travel season in Ireland
Master GPS navigation and tracker operation before the event; carry backup navigation tools and paper maps
Plan nutrition strategy carefully for self-supported format; identify resupply opportunities in villages along the Wild Atlantic Way route
Prepare for Atlantic coastal weather including wind, rain, and variable conditions; pack layered, weather-resistant gear
Connect with previous participants for route insights, pacing strategies, and logistical lessons learned
